transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Mason, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about my choices lately. You know, like, where I’m headed and all that. It’s just... there are so many options, and it’s hard to figure out what’s actually right for me. I mean, what if I make the wrong choice? It’s just... it’s overwhelming, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you're feeling quite overwhelmed by the weight of your choices and the uncertainty of making the right decision. Can you tell me more about what specific options are on your mind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, it’s just... there are a few things, like career paths and, I don’t know, personal stuff too. I mean, I’ve thought about changing jobs, but then I worry if it’s the right move. And then there’s, like, relationships, you know? Should I be more open to new people, or is it better to just stick with what I know? It’s like, every option has its own set of risks, and I just keep going in circles, thinking about it all. It’s frustrating, really.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re grappling with some significant decisions in both your career and personal life, and the uncertainty around those choices is really frustrating for you. When you think about changing jobs or exploring new relationships, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, when I think about changing jobs, I feel this knot in my stomach, like a mix of anxiety and excitement, I guess? But then there's also this fear of failure, like what if I make the wrong choice and end up regretting it? And with relationships, it’s kind of similar. I feel nervous about putting myself out there, but at the same time, I wonder if I'm missing out on something good. It’s just... a lot to juggle, and I don’t really know how to sort through it all.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you're experiencing a blend of anxiety and excitement when it comes to both your career and relationships, along with a fear of failure and missing out. That’s a lot to carry. Can you share more about what that knot in your stomach feels like, or how you usually cope when these feelings arise?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, the knot in my stomach... it’s like this tightness that just won’t go away, you know? It makes it hard to focus on anything else. I guess when I feel like this, I tend to just... overthink things. I’ll replay scenarios in my head, trying to figure out every possible outcome, but it just leads to more confusion. Sometimes I try to distract myself with other stuff, like watching shows or scrolling through my phone, but it doesn’t really help. I mean, it’s like I’m just avoiding the real issue, but I don’t know how to face it head-on. It’s frustrating, like I’m stuck in this loop.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that tightness in your stomach really impacts your ability to focus and leads you into a cycle of overthinking and avoidance. It must be really tough to feel stuck in that loop. When you think about facing these feelings head-on, what comes to mind for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, when I think about facing those feelings, I guess I feel a bit scared, to be honest. It’s like, what if I dig deeper and find something I don’t want to deal with? I mean, it’s easier to just keep everything at arm’s length, you know? But at the same time, I know that avoiding it isn’t really solving anything. I just... I don’t know how to start. It feels like I’d have to confront a lot of uncertainty, and that’s just daunting. I keep thinking maybe if I just wait a little longer, things will become clearer on their own, but I’m not sure that’s the right approach either. It’s just... a lot to think about.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like there's a real tension between wanting to avoid those deeper feelings and knowing that avoiding them isn’t helping you move forward. That fear of confronting uncertainty can be really daunting. When you think about waiting for things to become clearer, what do you hope might happen in that time?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, I guess when I think about waiting, I hope that maybe some of the options will just sort themselves out, you know? Like, maybe I’ll have a clearer idea of what I really want or what feels right. It’s like I’m just hoping for a sign or something that points me in the right direction. But then I also worry that by waiting, I might miss out on opportunities or let time slip away without making any progress. It’s this constant back-and-forth in my head, and I just don’t know if that’s a realistic way to approach it. I mean, I want to believe that clarity will come, but part of me is also scared that it won’t. It’s just... frustrating, like I’m caught in this limbo.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you're caught in a cycle of hope and worry—hoping that things will become clearer with time, but also fearing that waiting might lead to missed opportunities. That sense of being in limbo must feel really uncomfortable. Can you tell me more about what a "sign" would look like for you, or how you would know if you were moving in the right direction?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, when I think about a "sign," I guess it would be something that feels like a clear indication of what I should do next. Maybe it’s an opportunity that just feels right, or a conversation with someone that gives me a new perspective. I mean, I often imagine having this moment of clarity, like everything just clicks into place, and I know exactly what path to take. But then I wonder if I’m just setting myself up for disappointment, you know? What if those signs never come, or if I misinterpret them? It’s like I’m waiting for this big revelation, but in the meantime, I’m just... stuck. It’s frustrating because I want to feel confident in my choices, but the uncertainty just keeps hanging over me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’re longing for that moment of clarity, where everything feels aligned and you know the right path to take. Yet, there’s also a fear of disappointment if those signs don’t come or if you misinterpret them. That uncertainty can be really heavy to carry. When you think about the possibility of misinterpreting a sign or feeling stuck, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, when I think about misinterpreting a sign, I feel this wave of anxiety wash over me. It’s like, what if I make a choice based on something that isn’t even real? That thought just adds to the pressure, you know? I start to feel overwhelmed, like I’m just spiraling into doubt. It’s frustrating because I want to trust my instincts, but then I question everything. And being stuck, it just makes me feel helpless, like I’m not in control of my own life. It’s a mix of fear and frustration, and I wish I could just break free from it all.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like the fear of misinterpreting a sign brings on a wave of anxiety and a sense of overwhelm, which adds to your feelings of frustration and helplessness. That desire to trust your instincts while questioning everything must be really exhausting. When you think about feeling helpless or out of control, how does that impact your day-to-day life or the decisions you’re facing?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, feeling helpless really puts a damper on everything. It’s like I wake up each day with this weight on my shoulders, and it makes even the simplest decisions feel monumental. I find myself second-guessing everything, from what to eat for breakfast to bigger choices like job applications. It’s exhausting, honestly. I feel like I’m just going through the motions, but I’m not really living. I keep thinking about what I should be doing instead of just... doing it. And that just adds to the frustration. I mean, I want to feel like I have some control, but it’s hard when I’m constantly battling these thoughts. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle where I want to move forward, but I can’t seem to take that first step.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that sense of helplessness really weighs you down and makes even small decisions feel overwhelming. It must be incredibly frustrating to feel like you're just going through the motions without truly living. When you think about wanting to take that first step forward, what do you think is holding you back the most?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, I think what really holds me back is this fear of making the wrong choice. It’s like I’m paralyzed by the thought that whatever I decide could lead me down a path I don’t want to go. I keep thinking about all the potential consequences, and it just makes me freeze up. I mean, I know I should just take a leap and trust myself, but that voice in my head keeps saying, “What if you mess it up?” It’s like I’m stuck in this tug-of-war between wanting to move forward and being terrified of what that might look like. And then there’s the pressure to make the “right” decision, whatever that even means. It’s just... a lot to handle, and it makes it hard to see a clear way forward.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that fear of making the wrong choice really paralyzes you and creates a lot of pressure to find the “right” decision. That tug-of-war between wanting to move forward and feeling terrified of the consequences must be incredibly draining. When you think about that voice in your head that says, “What if you mess it up?” how does it make you feel about yourself and your ability to make choices?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, when I hear that voice in my head, it really chips away at my confidence. It makes me feel like I’m not capable of making good decisions, like I’m somehow flawed for even having these doubts. I start to question my judgment and wonder if I’m just not cut out for navigating life’s choices. It’s frustrating because I want to believe in myself, but that voice just keeps echoing, making me feel small and unsure. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop where I want to break free and trust my instincts, but I keep getting pulled back into that fear. And honestly, it just leaves me feeling defeated, like I’m not living up to my potential or something.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like that inner voice really undermines your confidence and makes you feel flawed for having doubts. It must be tough to feel like you're in a loop where you want to trust yourself but keep getting pulled back into fear. When you think about feeling defeated and not living up to your potential, what does that mean for you? How does that shape your view of yourself and your future?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, when I think about feeling defeated and not living up to my potential, it really hits hard. It’s like I have this vision of what I could be, you know? I see myself achieving things, making choices that lead to growth and fulfillment, but then I look at where I am and it feels like such a stark contrast. I start to think maybe I’m just not meant for those things, and that thought is really disheartening. It shapes my view of myself in a way that makes me feel like I’m just... stuck in this cycle of mediocrity. I worry that if I don’t break out of this, I’ll just end up living a life that feels unremarkable, and that scares me. I mean, I want to make an impact, to feel like I’m doing something worthwhile, but the fear of failing or not measuring up just looms over me. It’s like I’m constantly battling this internal narrative that tells me I’m not enough, and that makes it hard to see a hopeful future.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you have a strong vision of what you want to achieve and how you want to feel, but the contrast between that vision and your current experience is really disheartening for you. The fear of living a life that feels unremarkable and the internal narrative telling you that you're not enough must be incredibly heavy to carry. When you think about that vision of yourself achieving growth and fulfillment, what do you think it would take to start moving toward that?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, when I think about moving toward that vision, it feels like I need to find a way to quiet that inner critic, the one that keeps telling me I’m not enough. I guess it would take some kind of shift in mindset, maybe learning to embrace the idea that it’s okay to make mistakes along the way. But then I get stuck again, because I wonder how to actually do that. It’s like I need a plan or a step-by-step guide, but I also know that life doesn’t always work that way. I think maybe I need to start small, like setting little goals that feel achievable, but even that feels daunting sometimes. I just want to feel like I’m making progress, but the fear of not doing it right keeps creeping in. It’s frustrating because I know I have the potential, but figuring out how to tap into it feels like a puzzle I can’t solve.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you recognize that quieting that inner critic and embracing mistakes could be key to moving toward your vision, but figuring out how to do that feels daunting. The idea of starting small with achievable goals seems like a positive step, yet the fear of not doing it right still holds you back. When you think about those small goals, what kind of goals feel most achievable to you right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, I think the small goals that feel most achievable right now are probably the ones that don’t involve a lot of pressure or big decisions. Like, maybe just committing to spending a little time each day working on my website or even just jotting down some ideas for projects I want to pursue. It’s something I can control, and it feels less overwhelming than trying to tackle bigger life choices all at once. I guess I’m also thinking about setting a goal to reach out to someone for feedback on my work, which is a bit scary but could be helpful. It’s just that even these small steps come with their own set of worries—like what if I don’t get the response I hope for? Or what if I realize I’m not as good as I thought? It’s a bit of a balancing act, trying to find goals that feel manageable while also pushing myself a little. I just want to feel like I’m moving forward without the weight of those fears dragging me down.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Therapist: It sounds like you’ve identified some small, manageable goals that feel more within your control, like working on your website and reaching out for feedback. It’s understandable that even these steps come with their own worries about potential responses and self-doubt. When you think about the balancing act of pushing yourself while managing those fears, what do you think would help you feel more supported in taking those steps?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, I think what would really help me feel more supported is having someone to talk to about these goals and fears. Just having that accountability could make a difference, like knowing I’m not alone in this. I guess it would also be nice to have some encouragement along the way, someone who can remind me that it’s okay to stumble and that progress doesn’t have to be perfect. I mean, I tend to get caught up in my own head, and having an outside perspective might help me see things more clearly. Maybe even sharing my goals with a friend or a mentor could lighten the load a bit. It’s just hard to reach out sometimes, you know? I worry about burdening others with my struggles, but I also know that support can make a big difference. It’s just figuring out how to take that step to connect without feeling like I’m imposing.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having someone to talk to and share your goals with could really provide the support and accountability you’re looking for. It’s understandable to worry about burdening others, but it seems like you recognize the value of connection and encouragement. When you think about reaching out to a friend or mentor, what feelings come up for you? What might help you feel more comfortable taking that step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: Well, when I think about reaching out, I feel a mix of anxiety and hope, if that makes sense. There’s this nagging fear that I’ll be seen as needy or that my struggles will be too much for someone else to handle. I worry about how they might react, like what if they don’t have the time or the energy to support me? But then there’s also this flicker of hope that maybe they’ll understand and offer the encouragement I need. I guess it’s just that internal conflict between wanting to connect and fearing rejection or judgment. To feel more comfortable taking that step, I think it would help to remind myself that everyone has their own struggles, and reaching out doesn’t mean I’m imposing. Maybe if I framed it as just wanting to share my journey and get some feedback, it wouldn’t feel so heavy. But still, that leap feels daunting, and I’m not sure how to get over that initial hurdle.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re experiencing a real tug-of-war between anxiety and hope when it comes to reaching out. The fear of being seen as needy or imposing is understandable, especially when you’re already feeling vulnerable. It’s great that you’re considering reframing it as sharing your journey and seeking feedback, which might lighten the load. When you think about that initial hurdle, what do you think is the most challenging part for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, I think the most challenging part for me is just getting past that first step of actually reaching out. It’s like this mental block that feels so heavy. I start to overthink everything—what I’ll say, how they might respond, whether I’m bothering them. It’s almost like I build it up in my head to be this huge deal, when in reality, it might not be as big of a deal as I think. I guess I also worry about how I’ll feel after I reach out. What if it doesn’t go well? I know I shouldn’t let that stop me, but it’s hard to shake off those thoughts. I just want to feel like I’m making progress without the fear of what could go wrong hanging over me. It’s frustrating because I know that reaching out could be beneficial, but that initial leap feels like climbing a mountain.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that initial step of reaching out feels like a significant mental block for you, and the overthinking around it can amplify your fears. It’s understandable to feel frustrated, especially when you recognize the potential benefits of connecting with someone. When you imagine what it might feel like to take that leap, even if it feels daunting, what do you think could help you ease into that first step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, I think easing into that first step could really help if I could break it down into something even smaller. Like, instead of thinking about reaching out for a big conversation, maybe I could just send a quick message to say hi or share a small update about what I’m working on. That way, it feels less intimidating, and I wouldn’t have to dive into everything all at once. I guess it would also help to remind myself that it’s okay to start small and that the other person might appreciate hearing from me, even if it’s just a little check-in. I think if I could focus on the connection rather than the pressure of the conversation, it might take some of the weight off. But still, that little voice in my head keeps whispering doubts, making it hard to just take that first step. It’s like I’m caught in this loop of wanting to reach out but feeling paralyzed by the fear of how it might go.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like breaking it down into smaller steps, like sending a quick message, could make reaching out feel more manageable for you. Focusing on the connection rather than the pressure of a deeper conversation seems like a helpful shift in perspective. I hear you mentioning that persistent voice of doubt that makes it hard to take that first step. When you notice those doubts creeping in, what do you think they’re trying to protect you from?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, I think those doubts are trying to protect me from feeling vulnerable or facing rejection. It’s like they’re whispering that if I don’t reach out, I won’t risk getting hurt or being disappointed. But at the same time, I realize that by holding back, I’m also missing out on potential support and connection. It’s this weird paradox where the very thing that feels safe—staying quiet—also keeps me stuck. I guess those doubts are trying to keep me in my comfort zone, but I know deep down that growth often happens outside of that zone. It’s just frustrating because I want to push through, but that fear of what could go wrong is always lurking. It’s like I’m in this tug-of-war with myself, wanting to reach out but feeling held back by that protective instinct.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like those doubts are really trying to protect you from vulnerability and disappointment, which is a natural instinct. It’s understandable to feel that tension between wanting to stay in your comfort zone and recognizing that growth often requires stepping outside of it. When you think about this tug-of-war within yourself, what do you think it would feel like to acknowledge both sides—the desire for connection and the fear of rejection—without judgment? How might that shift your perspective on taking that first step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, acknowledging both sides feels a bit like giving myself permission to feel what I’m feeling without trying to push it away or judge it. It’s like recognizing that it’s okay to be scared and still want to connect. I think if I could sit with those feelings instead of trying to fight them, it might take some of the pressure off. I mean, it’s normal to have fears, right? Everyone does, and it doesn’t mean I’m weak or incapable. Maybe by accepting that fear is part of the process, I could approach reaching out with a little more compassion for myself. It could help me see that taking that first step doesn’t have to be perfect or free of anxiety. Just being honest about where I’m at might make it easier to move forward, even if it’s just a small step. It’s just that sometimes it feels like I’m stuck in this cycle of overthinking, and breaking that cycle is the hard part.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like giving yourself permission to feel both the fear and the desire for connection could really help you approach reaching out with more compassion and understanding. Acknowledging that it’s normal to have fears and that they don’t define your strength is an important insight. When you think about breaking that cycle of overthinking, what do you think might help you create space for those feelings without getting overwhelmed by them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, I think creating some space for those feelings might involve finding a way to express them without judgment. Maybe I could try journaling or even just talking to myself out loud, like a sort of mental rehearsal. It might help to get those thoughts out of my head and onto paper or into the air, so they don’t feel so heavy. I could also set aside a few minutes each day to just sit with my feelings, maybe even practice some mindfulness or breathing exercises to ground myself. That way, I could acknowledge the anxiety without letting it take over. I guess it’s about finding a balance—recognizing the fears but not letting them dictate my actions. It’s just tough because, in the moment, it’s easy to get swept away by those overwhelming thoughts. But if I can create that little bit of distance, maybe it would help me see things more clearly and take that first step without feeling so paralyzed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re considering some really constructive ways to create space for your feelings, like journaling, talking it out, or practicing mindfulness. Those strategies could help you acknowledge your anxiety without letting it control your actions. I hear you mentioning the challenge of getting swept away by overwhelming thoughts in the moment. When you think about those moments of feeling overwhelmed, what do you think would help you recognize when you're starting to feel that way, so you can gently remind yourself to use those coping strategies?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, I think recognizing those moments of overwhelm might start with paying attention to my physical cues. Like, maybe I notice my heart racing or my thoughts getting jumbled. If I can catch those signs early, it could be a signal for me to pause and take a breath. I guess it’s about being more in tune with myself and not just getting lost in the chaos of my mind. Maybe setting reminders on my phone to check in with myself throughout the day could help, too. Just a little nudge to take a moment and assess how I’m feeling. I think if I can create that awareness, it might make it easier to step back and use those strategies before I get too overwhelmed. But, of course, it’s easier said than done, especially when I’m in the thick of it. It’s like trying to find clarity in a storm.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like tuning into your physical cues could be a valuable way to recognize those moments of overwhelm before they escalate. Setting reminders to check in with yourself throughout the day seems like a practical approach to foster that awareness. I hear you acknowledging that it can be challenging to find clarity when you're in the midst of those overwhelming feelings. When you think about those moments of clarity, what do you think it would feel like to pause and take a breath, even if just for a moment, before reacting to those feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, pausing to take a breath in those moments could feel like a small but significant act of self-care. It’s like giving myself a little bit of space to regroup instead of just reacting impulsively to the anxiety. I think it would feel refreshing, almost like hitting a reset button, where I can step back and remind myself that it’s okay to feel overwhelmed. It might help me gain a bit of perspective, allowing me to see that these feelings don’t have to dictate my actions. I guess it could also be a moment to remind myself that I’m not alone in this struggle; everyone deals with their own challenges. But then again, there’s that nagging thought that I should be able to handle it all without needing that pause. It’s frustrating because I know I could benefit from that moment of clarity, but sometimes it feels like I’m fighting against my own expectations of how I should be coping.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like taking that pause could really serve as a refreshing moment of self-care and perspective for you. I hear you grappling with the expectation that you should be able to handle everything without needing that break, which can add to the frustration. When you think about those expectations, where do you think they come from, and how do they influence the way you respond to your feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, I think those expectations probably come from a mix of internal and external pressures. I mean, there’s this societal narrative that we should be able to manage everything on our own, like we’re supposed to have it all figured out. And then there’s my own voice in my head that tells me I should be stronger or more capable than I feel. It’s like I’ve built this idea of what success looks like, and if I don’t meet that standard, then I’m somehow failing. I guess it’s a way of trying to motivate myself, but it often backfires and just adds more weight to my shoulders. It’s frustrating because I want to be able to acknowledge that it’s okay to struggle, but then I get caught up in this cycle of feeling like I’m not doing enough. It’s like I’m constantly measuring myself against this invisible yardstick, and it’s exhausting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like those expectations are rooted in both societal pressures and your own internal narrative, creating a heavy burden for you. I hear you expressing frustration with the idea of success and how it can feel like an invisible yardstick that you’re constantly measuring yourself against. Acknowledging that it’s okay to struggle seems important, yet it feels challenging when you’re caught in that cycle of self-judgment. When you reflect on this cycle of feeling like you’re not doing enough, what do you think it would look like to redefine what success means for you personally, outside of those external standards?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Mason: You know, redefining success for myself feels like it could be a liberating process, but it’s also a bit daunting. I think it might involve shifting the focus from external achievements to more personal milestones—like recognizing my efforts, my growth, and even the small steps I take each day. Success could mean being honest with myself about where I’m at, rather than just chasing after some idealized version of what I think I should be. Maybe it’s about celebrating the moments when I reach out, even if it’s just a text to a friend, or acknowledging that I took time to pause and breathe instead of getting swept away by anxiety. It feels like I’d need to give myself permission to define success in a way that honors my journey, rather than just the end results. But then there’s that lingering fear that if I don’t have tangible accomplishments to show for it, I might still feel like I’m falling short. It’s like I’m caught between wanting to break free from those rigid definitions and the fear of what that might mean for my self-worth.

**Progression:**
